title: Data Center Grid Buildout — Electrical Contractors and Equipment Makers
summary: Today's $67B NextEra-Dominion merger validates the AI power buildout at the highest level — but the chokepoint isn't generation, it's the wires, transformers, and substations connecting it. PWR and ETN own that bottleneck.
catalyst: On May 18, 2026, NextEra Energy announced a $67B all-stock acquisition of Dominion Energy, creating the world's largest regulated utility with 110 GW of generation and 10 million customers across Florida, Virginia, and the Carolinas — anchoring the world's largest data center market in Northern Virginia [1][2]. The deal lands as US transformer lead times have extended to 4-5 years (from 24-30 months pre-2020), and demand for generator step-up transformers has surged 274% since 2019 [3]. The grid — not the chip — is now the binding constraint on AI compute, and the merger crystallizes a multi-year, multi-utility scramble for transmission, substations, and switchgear.
thesis: Investors keep buying the picks-and-shovels of generation (gas turbines, nuclear), but generation alone doesn't deliver electrons to a Virginia rack. Every gigawatt of new capacity also needs ~$300-500M of transmission, distribution, and substation work — which only a handful of contractors and equipment OEMs can deliver at scale. Quanta Services (PWR) is the largest US transmission contractor with a record $48.5B backlog and its data-center/load-center segment growing 70-110% in 2026 [4]. Eaton (ETN) is the leading electrical-equipment supplier with a 228 GW data center backlog — twelve years of 2025 build rates — and Q1 2026 data-center orders up 240% YoY [5][6]. Both are scarce, regulated-relationship businesses that consolidating utilities like the new NextEra-Dominion must lean on harder, not less, post-merger.
body_md:
## Setup

The May 18, 2026 announcement that NextEra Energy will acquire Dominion Energy in a $67 billion all-stock deal is the largest power-sector merger ever attempted [1][2]. NextEra shareholders will own 74.5% of the pro-forma company, Dominion shareholders 25.5%, with Dominion holders receiving 0.8138 NEE shares each. The combined entity will own roughly 110 GW of generation and serve approximately 10 million customers across Florida, Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina [2]. CEO John Ketchum was explicit about the strategic logic: only an entity of this scale can credibly finance and build the transmission and generation needed to satisfy hyperscaler demand. Closing requires FERC, NRC, and four state PUCs (12-18 month timeline).

The deal sits on top of an already-binding supply crunch in grid equipment. US large-power-transformer lead times have stretched to 4-5 years, versus 24-30 months pre-2020 [3]. Generator step-up (GSU) transformer demand is up 274% since 2019, and Cleveland-Cliffs is the sole domestic producer of the grain-oriented electrical steel transformer cores require [3]. The replacement-cycle math is unforgiving: most US large power transformers are 35-50 years old, and demand from data centers, manufacturing reshoring, and renewables is competing for the same shop hours.

## Why Now

Three threads converged this week:

1. **A utility CEO bet $67B that generation alone won't solve the problem.** NextEra already owns the largest US renewables fleet and one of the strongest nuclear portfolios. The fact that it's paying a premium to acquire Dominion — whose primary asset is *the wires running to Loudoun County* — tells you where the binding constraint actually is.
2. **The order books are screaming.** Eaton's Q1 2026 data-center backlog hit 228 GW [5][6], 12 years of build at 2025 rates. Quanta's $48.5B backlog [4] is up from $34B a year ago. These are not forecasts — they are signed contracts. Eaton's negotiation pipeline is up 81% on top of the backlog, which means the bookings are still accelerating.
3. **The bottleneck is structural, not cyclical.** Adding transformer capacity requires grain-oriented electrical steel, specialized magnet wire, multi-year furnace builds, and trained linemen — none of which respond to a price signal in under 2-3 years. Pricing power moves to whoever already has the capacity, the workforce, and the customer relationships.

## Company-Specific Edge

### Quanta Services (PWR)

Quanta is the largest specialty infrastructure contractor in North America with 69,500 employees, including a substantial IBEW-union skilled-trades workforce that is effectively impossible to recreate. The 2024 acquisition of Cupertino Electric made Quanta the dominant hyperscale data-center electrical contractor; Blattner Energy gave it the renewables EPC scale to chase utility-scale buildouts [4]. Q1 2026 results: revenue $7.87B (+26.3% YoY), record backlog $48.5B, book-to-bill 1.6x, technology/load-center segment guided to 70-110% revenue growth in 2026 [4][7]. Management called out the first meaningful 765 kV transmission award of the cycle — a class of project (e.g., American Electric Power's Cardinal Project) that effectively only Quanta and a handful of rivals can execute. FY2026 guidance was raised to $34.7-35.2B revenue and $13.55-14.25 adjusted EPS. The IOWN screener gives PWR a 75 with AI Resilience scored 9/10 — the work cannot be automated [screener].

### Eaton (ETN)

Eaton is the leading electrical-equipment supplier with $27.4B in 2025 revenue and a deliberate decade-long pivot away from hydraulics and toward power management [screener]. Q1 2026 was a record: revenue $7.5B, adjusted EPS $2.81, Electrical Americas revenue $3.6B (+20% YoY, +14% organic), trailing-12-month Electrical Americas orders +60%, and data-center orders alone up 240% YoY [5][6]. The headline number for the thesis: Eaton's total data-center backlog now stands at 228 GW, equivalent to 12 years of 2025 build rates [5]. Negotiation pipeline +81%. Bernstein raised its price target to $509 on the print. ETN scores 80 (BUY) in the IOWN screener with AI Resilience 9/10 and Infrastructure 9/10 — the financial profile is the cleanest in the peer set: ROE 21.5%, D/E 54.9%, 100 consecutive years of dividends [screener].

## Numbers That Matter

| Metric | PWR | ETN |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 Revenue | $7.87B (+26.3% YoY) | $7.50B (record) |
| Backlog | $48.5B (record) | 228 GW data-center alone |
| Data-center growth signal | Tech/Load segment +70-110% guide | Orders +240% YoY |
| Forward P/E | 46.0x | 25.8x |
| ROE | 13.5% | 21.5% |
| Revenue 5yr CAGR | ~20% | 9.8% |
| AI Resilience (IOWN) | 9/10 | 9/10 |
| Dividend track | None | 100 years paid, 16 years grown |

ETN is the cheaper, higher-quality balance sheet. PWR is the higher-beta, higher-growth play. They are not duplicative — Quanta builds the substation, Eaton sells the switchgear and UPS inside it.

## Bear Case

**Valuation.** PWR trades at 46x forward earnings; any deceleration in data-center bookings or a delay in 765 kV transmission projects will see multiple compression first. ETN's 25.8x forward P/E is less extreme but still richly priced for an industrial. If the AI capex narrative cracks — say, an NVDA miss this Wednesday or a hyperscaler pulling 2027 guidance — both names re-rate quickly.

**Margin pressure.** Eaton already guided 50 bps lower segment margins for 2026 on input-cost inflation and ramp expenses; April 1 price increases will take time to flow through. Quanta's GAAP profit margin sits at 3.7% — thin enough that project-execution problems on the new 765 kV programs could meaningfully impair earnings.

**Merger drag.** The NEE-D combination itself is a 12-18 month regulatory slog and could constrain Dominion's near-term capex while pending — though grid contractors usually keep working through M&A overhangs.

**Labor.** Quanta's growth depends on linemen and electrical journeymen who take 4-5 years to train. The workforce is the bottleneck no balance sheet can solve.

## Conclusion

The market is correctly excited about gas turbines and nuclear baseload (covered separately in the AI Power Bottleneck thesis). It is still under-appreciating that the same hyperscaler that ordered the turbines also has to order tens of thousands of distribution transformers and pay someone to physically string 765 kV lines from West Virginia to Loudoun County. Today's $67B utility merger is the loudest possible signal that the C-suites running the grid have decided scale and consolidation are required — and that means the contractors and equipment OEMs who already have the order book, the workforce, and the customer relationships get pricing power for the next 24-36 months. PWR and ETN sit at the top of that stack.

ticker_rationale.PWR: Record $48.5B backlog (vs $34B a year ago), book-to-bill 1.6x, and Technology/Load Center segment guided to 70-110% revenue growth in 2026. The first meaningful 765 kV transmission award entered backlog in Q1 — a project class with effectively no domestic substitute.
ticker_rationale.ETN: 228 GW data-center backlog (12 years of 2025 build rates), Q1 2026 data-center orders +240% YoY, Electrical Americas organic growth +14%, and a 25.6% segment operating margin. IOWN score of 80 (BUY) with ROE 21.5% and 100 consecutive years of dividends — quality at a sane multiple.

risks: An NVDA earnings miss or hyperscaler capex pullback (most likely trigger: NVDA print May 20, 2026) could re-rate the entire data-center supply chain, with PWR's 46x forward P/E most exposed.
Quanta's 3.7% GAAP profit margin leaves little room for project-execution problems on the new 765 kV transmission programs; a single high-profile cost overrun could swing FY EPS.
Eaton already guided 50 bps lower segment margins for 2026 on input-cost inflation; if April 1 price increases stall on competitive pressure (Schneider, ABB), margin compression worsens.
FERC/state regulators could impose ratepayer-protection conditions on the NEE-D deal that delay near-term capex, temporarily slowing the Dominion-territory work pipeline.
Skilled-trades labor shortage caps how fast Quanta can convert backlog to revenue — workforce constraints could mean the backlog gets paid out over 4-5 years rather than 2-3.
invalidation: ETN's trailing data-center order growth decelerates below +50% YoY in any quarter (currently +240%) — would signal the AI capex wave is cresting.
PWR backlog declines sequentially for two consecutive quarters from the $48.5B Q1 2026 peak — book-to-bill below 1.0x indicates demand is rolling.
Hyperscaler 2027 capex guidance comes in flat or down from 2026 levels (currently the consensus is for continued double-digit growth).
US large-power-transformer lead times shrink back below 30 months — would indicate the supply bottleneck has cleared and pricing power for OEMs has eroded.
PWR forward P/E compresses below 30x or ETN below 20x on no fundamental change — would suggest the secular narrative has broken.

sources.title: NextEra's $67 billion Dominion takeover creates world's largest utility — just in time to win the AI data center power surge
NextEra Energy to buy Dominion in deal that unites two key players in race to power AI data centers
U.S. transformer market faces severe supply constraints as lead times extend to four years
Quanta Services Posts Blowout Q1, Raises Full-Year Guidance as Record Backlog Surges to $48.5B
Eaton Posts Record Q1 Revenue as Electrical Backlog Surges 48% on Data Center Demand
Dominion-NextEra merger, fueled by AI data center demand, would create huge utility
